This course is structured into 6 modules, starting with a review of the ISO 19650-2 and its place in BIM according to ISO 19650. Then the course tackles the key parts of implementing BIM on a project – setting the information requirements, the execution plan response from the supply chain, how information is managed during the project, and what ISO 19650-2 says about the roles and responsibilities associated with information management.The course finishes with an action planning module, to help you start to plan your next steps in putting ISO 19650-2 into practice in your organization.This course consists of tutorials, delegate activities and discussions.UK: To highlight UK specific content and examples, a flag is used on the slides and italics in the notes. The UK implementation of ISO 19650 Part 2 is of interest in many other countries. Other localizations may be included.Your Delegate Workbook contains all printed materials related to this course, including slides, activities and reference materials. Sample answers and explanations to the activities are also in the back of your workbook. Please only refer to the answers for each activity after completing it, or if you’re really stuck.You will get the most out of this course by being an active participant, asking questions and engaging in discussions and activities. Remember there are no silly questions and please feel free to seek the help of your tutor as needed.Two short breaks will be taken at suitably convenient times in the morning and afternoon. 45 minutes will be given for a lunch break. Additional breaks may be taken as long as agreed by delegates and tutor, and all learning objectives are met. Course activities will be at the tutor’s discretion, depending on time and delegate needs. Finish times may therefore differ to those advertised.

This module provides a high-level introduction/reminder about ISO 19650-2, including its place in the family of ISO BIM standards and any other national standards or tools that apply in your country. It also provides a reminder of the concepts and principles of information management on a BIM project.
ISO 19650 Part 2: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Project Delivery Phase Training Course

BIM according to ISO 19650 is supported by a developing series of standards. There are now six parts in the published series, published between 2018 and 2025.These documents consist of: Concepts and principles for managing information over the whole asset lifecycle – ISO 19650-1:2018Information management during design and construction projects (called the delivery phase of assets) – ISO 19650-2:2018Information management during the operational phase of assets – ISO 19650-3:2020Information exchange including criteria for checking information quality – ISO 19650-4:2022Security-minded considerations for information management – ISO 19650-5:2020Other documents might exist at national level including formal National Annexes to ISO 19650-2 and ISO 19650-4.UK: The additional UK documents are described on the next slide, including the UK National Annex to ISO 19650-2.
ISO 19650 Part 2: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Project Delivery Phase Training Course
BS 8536:2022 Design, manufacture and construction for operability
UK BIM Framework Information Protocols for Part 2 and Part 3

UK: In the UK, the standard of information management required by the Government mandate and many other clients used to be called BIM Level 2. With the advent of the ISO 19650 standards this is being more generally termed as the UK BIM Framework (see www.ukbimframework.org). This is supported by a series of pillars that define and encapsulate the process and requirements. UK BIM implementation is guided by the use of the nine documents/tools on this slide:Concepts and principles for managing information over the whole asset lifecycle – BS EN ISO 19650-1:2018Information management during design and construction projects (called the delivery phase of assets) – BS EN ISO 19650-2:2018Information management during the operational phase of assets – BS EN ISO 19650-3:2020Information exchange including via COBie – BS EN ISO 19650-4:2022Security-minded considerations for information management – BS EN ISO 19650-5:2020Classification – Uniclass 2015 published and maintained by NBS (National Building Specifications)Delivering projects with operation and maintenance in mind, including Soft Landings – BS 8536:2022Health and safety information – ISO 1192-6:2018 (this has been developed into ISO 19650-6)Contractual supplement – UK BIM Framework Information Protocol templates (published 2020 and 2021)The full suite now exists within industry and therefore can be used to achieve UK BIM Framework using a mix of ISO and UK standards.The UK versions of the ISO standards (identified as BS EN ISO standards) include some supplementary material. All parts include short national forewords with updates to terminology and other matters. BS EN ISO 19650-2 and BS EN ISO 19650-4 each contain a UK National Annex. These national annexes contain some UK-specific details from the previous standards (BS 1192, PAS 1192-2 and BS 1192-4) that could not be included in the core ISO documents.

Principles of collaborative working to produce information containers

Originators produce information in models they control and checkClear definition of information requirementsEvaluation of the approach developed by supplier before contract awardA Common Data Environment (CDE) to manage and store informationTechnologies for developing information models conform to the ISOSecurity processes and procedures applied throughout the whole life of the asset

These 6 concepts and key principles are listed in Clause 9 of ISO 19650-1. They make clear that proper implementation of BIM according to ISO 19650 needs the involvement of both client/asset owner or operator (for items b and c) and the asset management or project delivery supply chain (for items a and f). In addition some resources are needed (items d and e).Everyone involved in a BIM project should be able to read down this list and confirm that all these concepts/principles are being applied. If any of them are missing, then the asset management or the project delivery cannot comply with ‘BIM according to ISO 19650’.These principles underline why client statements on their own such as “deliver this project using BIM” are unsatisfactory. Of course it is possible to apply BIM principles only within the supply chain, but many of the benefits of BIM will not be achieved in these cases.As this is the first time the phrase ‘information container’ has been used in the slides, it is worth considering the definition: An information container is defined in ISO 19650-1 (3.3.12) as: ‘A named persistent set of information retrievable from within a file, system or application storage hierarchy’Containers are repositories for information. They can be drawings, schedules, models, or sub-sets of files such as chapters or layers. Any system of storing the information in a single place is a container. Naming of containers therefore must be agreed on all projects – this is covered in later in the course.

ISO 19650-2 also follows the typical structure. There are normative and informative parts of the text – normative text sets out the requirements while informative text provides additional information.The normative references and the terms and definitions give the indispensable external references for understanding and implementing the ISO, and the definitions used specifically within Part 2. These are in addition to the definitions contained in ISO 19650-1 which are automatically inherited by it being a normative reference.The scope reiterates that this standard applies to projects and to all the different organizations engaged in commissioning or delivering a project.Clause 4 sets out the high-level process for managing information on a project. The individual steps within this process are then defined more specifically within the sub-clauses in Clause 5.

This BIM maturity model articulates the differences between working in a BIM Environment and previous ways of working. The diagram illustrates how BIM brings together different aspects – the layers – and how these become more sophisticated as the stage of BIM implementation increases.UK: Stage 2 BIM is equivalent to what used to be BIM Level 2 and is now called the UK BIM Framework. This is the level of maturity mandated by the UK Government for its projects in its Information Management Mandate (published in the ‘Transforming Infrastructure Performance Roadmap to 2030’.The maturity definitions can be summarized as follows: Stage 1: File based collaboration in 2D or 3D format using a collaboration tool providing a common data environment, possibly some standard data structures and formatStage 2: Managed 3D environment where separate information models are federated (combined) into checkable formats. Integration is on the basis of open standards where possible, but proprietary interfaces or bespoke middleware might also be used or needed. Stage 2 explicitly brings together the project delivery and the asset operation activities into a holistic information management processStage 3: Information management through database technologies using object servers to provide a common resource for all project and asset information management activities. Many aspects of this maturity of BIM are still being developedISO 19650-1 and -2 span across all stages of BIM implementation, though many opportunities for efficacy and efficiency are missing from stage 1, and stage 3 may require closer integration of processes.

Structure of the project team in ISO 19650-2

This diagram represents figure 2 from ISO 19650-2. The language used to describe the different organisations involved in a project team is deliberately generic (not using terms like client, contractor, sub-contractor) so as not to make assumptions about how design and construction work is organised in different countries.ISO 19650 identifies three types of organisation involved in information management:‘Appointing party’, labelled A at the centre of the figure, represents the organization who is the principal recipient of information generated during the project. This party is often called the client. There is only one appointing party for a particular project‘Lead appointing party’, labelled B in the middle ring of the figure, represents one of the direct appointments made by A. In projects, these organisations might be design consultants, project manager, cost consultant, main contractor, etc. There will be at least one lead appointed party on a project, but often there will be more than one. There is no limit to how many Bs there are – the only criterion is that each lead appointed party is appointed directly by the appointing party‘Appointed party’, labelled C around the edge of the figure, represents one of the organisations or internal teams appointed by each B to produce the actual information that is being asked for by the appointing party. An appointed party can be part of the same organisation that is also the lead appointed party, but can also be an external organisation to B. There is no limit to the number of Cs that can be appointed by each BIn addition to the types of party (A, B, C), the figure also illustrates the sub-divisions of the project team:‘Delivery team’ is made up of one lead appointed party and their own appointed parties. A delivery team can be as large or as small as necessary to produce the required information and the appointed parties within a delivery team can be arranged into a complex, multi-layer hierarchy if this is appropriate‘Task team’ is a sub-division of a delivery team that is focused on producing one particular aspect of the information deliverables that the lead appointed party is responsible for. Often the task teams will be the same as the appointed parties but this does not have to be the caseFor example, one appointed party might comprise several task teams such as a multi-disciplinary design firm with separate teams covering architecture, structural engineering, building services engineering. Or, one task team might comprise several appointed parties such as a team responsible for information relating to Bridge X on a road project where the task team would bring together the geotechnical consultant, civil/structural engineering designer, bridge construction contractor, street-lighting installation specialist.

The Information Delivery Cycle (IDC): Setting requirements

The first detailed module of the course introduces the project Information Delivery Cycle (IDC). As we shall see, there is a lot more to this diagram than may at first meet the eye.The first aspect of the IDC that we shall investigate is the way that an appointing party (UK: Client) understands its own needs for project-related information, and how it asks for this information from its supply chains.Even if you are not the appointing party (UK: Client) on a project, it is still important to understand the thought process that they have to go through to develop their information requirements.Also, as the need to define requirements is a task that cascades down supply chains, there are many more organizations than just the client who need to do this. Such as main contractors with sub-contract supply chains, and the intermediate levels of sub-contractor in those supply chains. Also design consultants who are employing specialists to carry out specific aspects of the design work, such as acoustic specialists or façade specialists.

Overview of the information management process (ISO 19650-1 Figure 11)This diagram summarizes the information management process captured in the concepts and principles of ISO 19650-1. These are implemented at project level through the requirements listed in ISO 19650-2 and at asset/facility management level through the requirements in ISO 19650-3.There are several elements to this diagram:The inner green wedge shape: This indicates that information quantity grows during a project (as it progresses through its plan of work stages) and during asset management (as trigger events prompt collection of new and updated information). It also indicates that a project should always start with some information, and that when the project is complete not all the project information model will be relevant or needed for asset managementThe outer blue pathway: This indicates that the process of making any of the appointments goes through a structured process of defining requirements, reviewing the capability and capacity of those being considered for the work, mobilization of each delivery team, and then production of the information itselfThe delivery of information to the appointing party: This comes from delivery team decisions (the green diamonds) and is exchanged with the appointing party (green circles) with before and after reviews for completeness and quality (green circular arrows). The information is used by the appointing party or its stakeholders to make their key decisions regarding the project or the assetPlease see your delegate materials for a larger version of the diagram.

Stages 0 and 7 connect the project to the broader activity of asset management
Project stages used in the UK are defined in the Unified Plan of Work (also RIBA Plan of Work 2020
For both built estates and infrastructure

Delivery of information during a project is tied back to the way that project is divided up into manageable parts. Mostly this is done by reference to a Plan of Work, which defines a sequence of stages that cover the whole life of a constructed asset (design, construction and operation/use). UK: The Plan of Work is either known as the CIC (Construction Industry Council) Unified Plan of Work or the (Royal Institute of British Architects) RIBA Plan of Work. The latter is probably better known, particularly in projects concerning buildings. The current version of the Plan of Work was published in 2020. This presentation is based on RIBA material. Of the eight stages, numbered 0 to 7, stages 1-6 represent the project process. Stage 7 represents the operational life of the building or asset, and stage 0 represents the strategic discussion concerning an existing asset or a new need that might give rise to a new project. Stage 7 has the longest duration (usually many decades), and stage 0 might be revisited periodically (for example every three or four years) on a number of occasions before a new project is identified and taken forward.Of course, a client may choose to define their own project/asset management lifecycle stages, and to base their BIM implementation on that instead.

Information during a project is a dynamic concept. It grows as the project is being progressed and delivered, through design and during construction. The area inside the blue line shows growth being steady, but this is just a simplification in the diagram. There are two handover points at the beginning and at the end of a project. The handover at the beginning represents use of pre-existing information – information about a building that is to be refurbished, or about a site that is to be developed. The handover at the end is from the project to asset/facility management.The information model during project delivery is the Project Information Model – this comprises graphical information (3D objects arranged geometrically, with some data attached to those objects), non-graphical data in addition to what is carried by each object, and documentation relating to objects, systems or the project as a whole.After handover, some of the contents of the Project Information Model might be used for long-term asset/facilities management as part of the Asset Information Model. Project information that is not used in the Asset Information Model can be retained for record purposes. When specified and handed over correctly, project information used for asset management provides a significant saving of time and money, since the asset surveys and information gathering does not have to be repeated at the commencement of facilities management.

The procurement of information needs to be planned in detail, and in advance. This applies to both projects and to asset management, but we will only consider the project implications here as defined in ISO 19650-2.It starts with the appointing party deciding what project information is needed for each of the key project management gateways or decision points that the project has to pass through. Usually these are aligned with Plan of Work stages, but they do not have to occur at every stage. There might also be a different set of stakeholders who need information at each decision point – depending on the nature of the decision and when it takes place during the project.A client requirement for information to support a key decision point means that there has to be a formal information exchange from the delivery team leading up to that decision (in terms of project stages, this will be at the same time). The information being exchanged will be collated from whoever is actively working on the project during the lead up to that information exchange.These potentially different supply chain arrangements are referenced by blue arrow going from left to right below the green wedge. For example, the appointing party might appoint one delivery team to provide a scheme master-plan and draw up a project brief (UK: Stage 1), might appoint a multi-disciplinary design team to develop concepts and outline designs (UK: Stage 2 and Stage 3), and then might appoint a design and build contractor to continue the project through to handover (UK: Stage 4, 5 and 6).Each of these appointments has to be procured with respect to the information expected to be delivered. This procurement process is represented by the blue arrows across the top of the diagram and down the left hand side. We will talk about each of the steps in this process in more detail in later slides.
ISO 19650 Part 2: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Project Delivery Phase Training Course
Asset Information Model (AIM)Project Information Model (PIM)

This slide shows Figure 2 from ISO 19650-3. This is an evolution of the requirements hierarchy shown in ISO 19650-1 Figure 2. It shows how the different types of information requirements relate to each other in general terms. We can see that there are four types of information requirements. ISO 19650-2 directly relates to Project Information Requirements and Exchange Information Requirements, but also acknowledges the influential role played by Organizational Information Requirements (OIR) and Asset Information Requirements (AIR).Project Information Requirements represent the appointing party’s need for information to make sure the project delivers against its objectives. Some of these will be concerned with running a good project, and others will be concerned with how the building or infrastructure will be operated and maintained during its life, and how it will support the organisational objectives of the appointing party (for example to deliver public services, to house manufacturing facilities, to provide workspace for office staff).The Exchange Information Requirements are detailed specifications of exactly what information is needed and this forms part of the work-specification for the lead appointed parties being engaged on the project. The results are the information deliverables which collectively form the Project Information Model. From the project information, relevant information can then be transferred across to the Asset Information Model, to support through-life operation and maintenance of the asset.

The appointing party (UK: Client) also needs to prepare other relatively stable resources/documentation to manage the information processes. Indeed, appointing parties should only set out specific requirements where they absolutely need to, for example to force the supply chain to deliver information exchanges in a format compatible with existing client IT systems. To over-specify the requirements will reduce the ability of the supply chain to innovate and deliver the most economical solution.These documents are covered in Clause 5.1 of ISO 19650-2, including:Project information requirements (5.1.2)Decisive informationPurposes for informationProject information milestones (5.1.3): The information delivery milestones set out the timetable for when information is required – these are the appointing party's own milestones but of course they could be adjusted to reflect the response from the delivery teams, once appointments start being madeKey decision points Project Information Standard (5.1.4): The project information standard is the place where the appointing party (client) sets out how it wants information to be exchanged between the various parties on the project, how it wants information to be structured and classified, how it assigns level of information need, and how information will be used in the operation of the assetClassification (facility, systems, products, tasks) and breakdown structures (asset, work, cost) Level of information needNeed for operational and long term information

Project information production methods and procedures (5.1.5) The project information production methods and procedures allows the appointing party to specify rules for how delivery teams need to produce their information. This includes how existing asset information is to be captured, how information is generated, reviewed and approved, how security is maintained, and how information is actually to be delivered to it.Capture of existing informationAuthoring, generation and reviewOutput, delivery Project information references (5.1.6) the project reference information and shared resources are the information starting points that the appointing party is providing to its delivery teams. These include any existing asset information (from a range of sources), particular resources such as templates for procedural documents e.g.  BIM Execution Plan (BEPs), information containers (e.g. data schedules), and style or object libraries that appointed parties are to use (including national libraries).Existing informationAdjacent propertiesTemplates and librariesProject Information Protocol (5.1.8) The project information protocol is where the appointing party sets out the legal/contractual framework for the production and delivery of information. This could include the rights, obligations and liabilities of the parties involved, as well as any licenses being provided relating to the use of the information and the use of tools used in its production.ObligationsLicencesWarranties and liabilitiesIntellectual property rightsUK: The Information protocol template published as part of the UK BIM Framework is provided as an example in the References.Also within Clause 5.1 is the provision of the Common Data Environment (5.1.7), as well as the functionality the CDE needs to have. This was covered in the Fundamentals course and there are some additional details in module 3.The last part of the documentation that the appointing party has to provide is the Exchange Information Requirements for each appointment it is making – these are discussed in more detail in the following slides. As we’ve discussed previously the EIR will contain information relevant to the information requirements. Statements within the EIR must be aligned to the PIRs and/or the Asset Information Requirements. The contents of an EIR are specific to the project roles, stage and any additional purposes.

High-level information requirements are called Project Information Requirements (PIR). They are important to technical and non-technical stakeholdersThey are related to the project approval gateways:Agreement that a project is neededSelecting the preferred concept designConfirming the construction costAccepting the completed asset

The starting point for the appointing party (UK: Project client) is to understand their own high-level needs for project information. Ultimately this will be client-specific, but there are likely to be a number of areas of interest that all clients have in common. For example, clients will be interested in adherence to budget, delivery of operational outcomes against the technical needs that created the project in the first place, compliance with relevant legislation or corporate standards. Some clients may have a particular interest in the delivery date (e.g. a board of school governors, or a supermarket), or the long-term running costs of the building/asset. Other clients may be concerned about the environmental impacts of the project.These information requirements come partly from the client's own project governance procedure – what questions need to be answered within the project delivery team at successive gateways – and partly from the business interests of other departments such as finance, legal, operations, estates management, and so on. Once they are understood, these requirements may only change rarely so do not need to be written from scratch every time a new project is being considered.In the language of ISO 19650 these high-level requirements for project information are called Project Information Requirements (PIRs). As they are linked to the specific project decisions and approvals that the client will apply internally to keep the project on track, they are almost certain to be linked to the client's own set of key project stages.

The Project Information Requirements are taken into account when the appointing party is defining the Exchange Information Requirements for a particular lead appointed party, but there is no requirement in ISO 19650-2 for the PIRs to be issued to the lead appointed party (UK: design consultant, contractor, etc). Note that information needed to operate and maintain the asset is really part of the Asset Information Requirements, but these will still feed through into the EIR as necessary to make sure that project appointments will deliver the information needed by asset and facilities managers.

The PIRs documented by an appointing party (UK: Project client) will be helpful to them internally to see that a project is addressing their key concerns, but these high-level information needs on their own are unlikely to be precise enough to put directly into any of the contracts with their delivery teams (UK: Designers, contractors etc).More specific information requirements are needed for this, and these are the Exchange Information Requirements (EIRs). EIRs exist at the level of each individual appointment that is made to deliver the whole project. There will be a wide range of these, between the appointing party (UK: Client) and their designers (architect, structural engineer, building services engineer, etc.), between the client and their contractor, between the contractor and their sub-contractors, etc.In theory EIRs will cascade all the way down the supply chain. Of course they should be getting more and more specific (and concise) as they do this, as each tier of sub-contracts deals with smaller and smaller segments of the project.

The Exchange Information Requirements for any particular appointment bring together some different elements. These all need to be thought about by the appointing party, but the extent to which they are specified will depend on the context, complexity and/or scale of the project and the appointment within it.Each EIR should consider the level of information need – the description of how complex, detailed or “rich” the information deliverable needs to be. There might be existing accepted standards for this, such as a standard definition of what is to be contained in a particular type of technical report.The acceptance criteria for the EIR need to be described. These should cross-reference the information standard and the information production methods and procedures (but there may be very specific criteria for a particular EIR that need to be explained in addition to these reference documents).The supporting information for the EIR has to be made available, especially as this is being provided by the appointing party to help the lead appointed party produce the information being asked for.Finally, the delivery deadlines need to be set out. In setting achievable deadlines, the appointing party will need to take account of how long is needed to carry out its review and acceptance processes.

EIRs can be documented through a standardized schedule, such as the one shown on this slide. ISO 19650-2 does not dictate any particular layout or structure of such a schedule, but it would have to capture all the necessary elements of an EIR statement. Additional details such as how the information is going to be provided, come as part of the information planning by the appointed party.The breakdown of EIRs according to lead appointed party (recipient) will also reflect the appointing party’s key decision points and the actual timing of these. These will also be connected to the project Plan of Work and the schedule of the different project stages. This slide shows how a single entry in an EIR can express a concise idea. For a single appointment, the schedule of EIRs can run to many rows, each describing a different deliverable that is expected from the lead appointed party.

The idea of cascading information requirements down the supply chain is central to ISO 19650-2. Clause 5.2.1 describes that the appointing party (UK: Project client) has to establish the Exchange Information Requirements for each appointment it is making. In the example of this slide, there are four appointments made by the appointing party, so there are four sets of EIRs.Clause 5.4.3 then describes that each lead appointed party cascades some of the appointing party's EIRs down to its appointed parties. At the same time, the lead appointed party can add its own EIRs, for example if it needs specific information to help it manage the delivery team. These deliverables will not be shared with the appointing party (UK: Project client).There is also the matter of making the EIRs at any particular level of the supply chain appropriate to the complexity and scope of the work at that level. So it is good practice for any organization to make sure that the EIRs they issue to their different suppliers are targeted just to the information deliverables that each supplier is responsible for. If an appointing party takes the easy route just to issue a project-wide EIR document to every lead appointed party, then this does not help the recipient to clearly see what they are being made responsible for. This can easily turn into risk-shedding, which is not compatible with the collaborative working principles behind BIM.

How can one single PIR be broken down into a complex sequence of separate information requirements at different levels of a project team?

It is also important to understand how one single PIR might need to be broken down into a complex sequence of separate information requirements at different levels of a project team.In the example on this slide and notes below, the appointing party is interested in the overall revenue from a new rail infrastructure project, and seeks this information from his project manager. The project manager breaks this down into separate requirements aimed at the track designer (to understand how fast trains will be able to travel and therefore how many trains per day will be running), the train manufacturer (to understand how many passengers each train could carry), and the transport consultant (to understand what the passenger demand might be and what level of fares might be charged).Client PIR: Does train system revenue meet business plan?EIR to Project manager: Confirm the projected revenue per day at the end of Developed Design (Stage 3 in the UK)PM -> Track designer: Maximum speed of trainTrack designer -> Signalling designer: Safe distance between trainsTrack designer -> Civil engineer: Maximum gradient of trackTrack designer -> Land acquisition: Ownership of land on train pathPM -> Train manufacturer: Capacity of trainTrain manufacturer -> Seat designer: Spacing of seats, percentage standingPM -> Transport consultant: Revenue per passengerTransport consultant -> Customer analyst: Customer profile, price elasticityTransport consultant -> Competitor analyst: Competing operators on routeOf course, not every PIR will require such a complex breakdown of information from the project team.

The Information Delivery Cycle (IDC): Delivery team response

This module is about how each supplier responds to the information requirements they have received from the appointing party or lead appointed party (their client) in the PIRs, EIRs, and any associated project BIM documentation.

After they have organized their own resources to carry out their information management activities (the information management function), the first stage of a BIM response from a lead appointed party to an appointing party is to prepare the pre-appointment BIM Execution Plan (BEP).The pre-appointment BEP is a document where the prospective lead appointed party (UK: Lead supplier) can do three specific things on behalf of the delivery team they lead:They can explain in broad terms how the delivery team is going to meet the technical, management and commercial requirements of the PIR and EIRThey can respond to any requests for information management proposals identified in the EIRThey can explain the team’s capability and capacity to work on this BIM projectAs its name suggests, the pre-appointment BEP is part of a tender return, so it is prepared while the supplier is still trying to win the project work. Because of this, the amount of effort that goes into completing the pre-appointment BEP needs to be managed carefully. Some parts of the BEP will be project specific, and will clearly need to be created or edited each time a pre-appointment BEP is issued. But other parts of the BEP will be more stable over time and can become standard corporate documents.Where a pre-qualification process is also used, before tenders are issued to shortlisted suppliers, then some of the issues around the project information standard or the project information production methods and procedures might be addressed through pre-qualification (e.g. use of CDE). If this is the case, then of course these should not need to be asked and answered again.The contents of the pre-appointment BEP are described in ISO 19650 Part 2, Clause 5.3.2. It is important to understand that these are identified as items to be considered for inclusion and they should only be part of the pre-appointment BEP if they are needed in the context of the project and the appointment:a) Professional résumé of the individuals proposed for any information management functions b) The delivery team’s information delivery strategyc) The proposed federation strategy to be adopted by the delivery teamd) The delivery team’s high-level responsibility matrix, and the key deliverables associated to each elemente) Any proposed additions or amendments to the project’s information production methods and procedures f) Any proposed additions or amendments to the project’s information standardg) A proposed schedule of information technology the delivery team intend to adopt.The “shall consider” approach is used many times throughout ISO 19650-2 as a way of introducing some project-specific flexibility in the extent of the appropriate actions for many of the core requirements of the standard

Infrastructure federation strategy and the resulting container breakdown structure
Key part of managing collaborative project design
Federation strategy spells out who has “ownership” of the jigsaw defining the project

The federation strategy describes how the project, in this case an infrastructure project, as a whole is to be divided up as a container breakdown structure so as to render the project (or asset) manageable. This can be for a number of reasons:Managing parallel design processes associated with different design tasks – these are often aligned with design disciplines but could also be aligned with particular project elements such as a different systemsIn this instance, each container breakdown would describe the 3D space needed by each discipline for their designManaging information access for security reasons – here volumes would be defined according to which parts of the design are restricted to certain members of the teamManaging the flow of information over the internet – particularly where the IT infrastructure is not suitable to handle transfer of whole-project model files quickly enough to allow effective remote workingWhatever the reason, each individual container breakdown must not overlap with any other container breakdown and each part of the project must exist in one container. There must be no gaps or duplication between the containers. Once containers are agreed, the definitions should be communicated to all members of the team. Container naming should be one of the project information standard or information production methods/procedures, so that everyone who is generating project information and is able to name their files correctly.
ISO 19650 Part 2: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Project Delivery Phase Training Course
Infrastructure federation strategy and the resulting container breakdown structure
Key part of managing collaborative project design
Federation strategy spells out who has “ownership” of the jigsaw defining the project

The federation strategy describes how the project, in this case a building project, as a whole is to be divided up as a container breakdown structure so as to render the project (or asset) manageable. This can be for a number of reasons:Managing parallel design processes associated with different design tasks – these are often aligned with design disciplines but could also be aligned with particular project elements such as a lift/stair/riser/structural coreIn this instance, each volume would describe the 3D space needed by each discipline for their designManaging information access for security reasons – here volumes would be defined according to which parts of the design are restricted to certain members of the teamManaging the flow of information over the internet – particularly where the IT infrastructure is not suitable to handle transfer of whole-project model files quickly enough to allow effective remote workingWhatever the reason, each individual container breakdown must not overlap with any other container breakdown and each part of the project must exist in one container. In other words there must be no gaps or duplication between the containers. Once containers are agreed, the definitions should be communicated to all members of the team. Container naming should be one of the required fields in the name convention, so everyone who is generating project information and naming files has to be able to apply the correct container code to their files.

The capability and capacity of each task team is assessed as part of the development of the tender response.This looks for capability and capacity in three specific areas:The task team’s capability and capacity to manage informationThe task team’s capability and capacity to produce informationThe availability of information technology (IT) within the task teamAs in ISO 19650-2 Clause 5.3.3 and 5.3.4.The type of information asked for is not likely to change very quickly. So this is likely to become one of your standard corporate documents, perhaps updated on an annual basis.Understanding the BIM capability of your own organization needs to become part of your general organizational management process – in just the same way that you manage your technical capabilities.If your organization is a lead appointed party (UK: Tier 1 supplier to a client) then you should add BIM capability to your usual supply chain management practices so that most of the required assessments from your delivery team can be assembled quickly and easily into a summary to be provided to the appointing party (client).

Delivery team mobilization plan and information risk register: ISO 19650-2 Clauses 5.3.5 and 5.3.6

The final parts of the tender response from each lead appointed party are the mobilization plan and the information management risk register.The mobilization plan explains how the delivery team is going to mobilize its resources, on the assumption that it is successful in winning the tender. ISO 19650-2 Clause 5.3.5 includes a long list of considerations to be included in this plan. These include:How the information production methods and procedures are going to be documented and testedHow the team will test its access to the CDEHow any shortfall in training amongst the team will be addressedHow additional resources to provide the required capacity will be found (either by recruitment, training or out-sourcing)The risk register mentioned in ISO 19650-2 Clause 5.3.6 is concerned only with information management risks. It is important that these are documented and managed, in the same way that other types of risk will be managed by the lead appointed parties (such as risks from bad weather or from lack of materials). A number of topics to be considered as potential information management risks are listed in ISO 19650-2 Clause 5.3.6, including:Risks around information delivery milestonesRisks transferred to the lead appointed party in the Information ProtocolRisks from not accepting any of the lead appointed party’s suggested changes to the Project Information Standard.

This feeds into the development of the post-appointment BEP which builds on the pre-appointment BEP previously submitted. But, particularly if the procurement process has taken a long time, or has prompted some rethinking by the client, there might be a lot of changes required.There are several aspects to this more detailed planning which are either not possible or sensible to do prior to appointment (UK: Contract award). These include: Preparing the detailed responsibility matrix (5.4.2)Incorporating information requirements from the lead appointed party in the other delivery team appointments (5.4.3)Preparing the task and master information delivery plans (5.4.4 and 5.4.5)Mobilizing the team resources (5.5.1), the information technology (5.5.2), and testing the project’s information production methods and procedures (5.3.5)These aspects of the BEP are covered on the following slides.

Whereas the high-level responsibility matrix explains in broad terms where the responsibility for producing information is placed, the detailed matrix takes this to the next stage of development. This sets out in more detail the mechanics of how information production is being allocated and co-ordinated among all those in the delivery team.The high-level responsibility matrix is the plan that leads the individual task teams to generate their own task information delivery plans.

Information requirements from the lead appointed party

The lead appointed party (UK: Tier 1 design consultant or Tier 1 contractor) has the opportunity to incorporate additional information requirements when further members of the delivery team are appointed. These add to the cascade of appointing party (client) information requirements explained earlier.

Typically, these additional information requirements are used by a main/lead contractor who will need more frequent information exchanges within the delivery team to make sure the project work is being properly controlled. There may also be construction specific information requirements (such as temporary works design information or site traffic management details) that might not be an information requirement for the appointing party (UK: Client).Apart from this, information requirements from the lead appointed party are treated in exactly the same way as those from the appointing party – levels of information needed and acceptance criteria need to be defined, as well as delivery dates.

Task Information Delivery Plans (TIDP) are prepared by each task team, which broadly equates to each design discipline, or key contractor package. As the TIDP refers to everything produced by one team, this document as a whole will define who is delivering each of the deliverables it lists. It is helpful if each TIDP is generated to the same format – spreadsheet layout or Gantt project plan so that TIDPs from different teams can be combined into a single plan as easily as possible.Each task team shall establish a TIDP, and maintain this plan throughout its appointment. In doing this, each task team has to consider:The project’s information delivery milestonesThe task team’s responsibilities within the detailed responsibility matrixThe lead appointed party’s information requirementsThe availability of shared resources within the delivery team; andThe time the task team will need to produce (generate, coordinate, review and approve) informationThe TIDP needs to include:Details of each information deliverable Not just 3d models Derivatives from 3d models (plans, elevations, sections) But also other deliverables such as schedules, documents and drawings such as schematics which might not be able to be generated from the 3d modelling toolDetails of when it is going to be delivered – the first iteration of the plan might just refer to the relevant project stage and the planned delivery date for that stage. Later iterations of the plan should provide more precise delivery dates that take account of the sequential logic of how deliverables are producedMore details are on the next slide.

Example fields in:Master Information Delivery Plan (MIDP)Task Information Delivery Plan (TIDP)

The TIDP shall list and identify, for each information container:the name and title;  the predecessors or dependencies; the level of information need  the (estimated) production duration  the information author responsible for its production; and  the delivery milestones

The TIDP has to include the following for each information container:The name including sequence/series number and title/descriptionThe predecessors or dependenciesSuch as background and prerequisite information containersThe level of information needEspecially from any additional purposes (implied objects, relationships, attributes needed)The information author responsible for its production: (RACI names and roles where R=responsible, A=accountable, C=consulted, I= informed)The (estimated) production duration; and the delivery milestones for example: First WIP (may depend on the last sharing of prerequisites)First sharing of the containerFirst publication (may depend on last publication of prerequisites)It is also good practice to indicate how the information aligns to the federation strategy and information container breakdown structure. This is not specifically mentioned in the standard, but could be conveyed in terms of:(Sub)-project/facilitySystem scope Spatial scopeIt is a matter for the appointing party (UK: Client) to dictate via the EIR if they require a particular level of detail or information which is outside of what would normally be expected at a certain project stage (or in-use event). For example, the marketing department of a five star hotel chain might have a PIR about the quality of public areas in a new hotel, which might lead to an EIR directed at the architect for greater specification/detail of key fixtures and fittings at an earlier stage than would be usual.Otherwise the appointing party would expect the supplier to propose the levels of detail and information that they are intending to provide within the level of information need framework that the appointing party has defined. UK: For example:Level of Detail (LoD) to describe the geometry richness of the model (how much drawn detail is in each object)Level of Information (LoI) to describe the data richness of the model (how much attribute data is attached to each object)

Combine all task plans into a single plan for each delivery team

Once task information delivery plans have been provided by the task teams, these are combined into a Master Information Delivery Plan (MIDP). As an aggregation of TIDPs, each MIDP contains the same types of detail as a TIDP.

There are as many MIDPs as there are lead appointed parties, though the appointing party will need an overview. One common approach, particularly in the building sector, is to have two organizations providing the information management role, one for the design-led phase and another for the construction-led phase. In these cases there will be two MIDPs.The preparation and maintenance of the MIDP is the responsibility of the lead appointed party.It is particularly important that the lead appointed party reviews the logic between information deliverables from different task teams to make sure that a logical sequence of information production is set out. It is also important to take account of the time required to federate and review the information model, so that delivery dates are set far enough ahead of team-wide discussions. Where a set of information deliverables is also being presented to the appointing party for their review and acceptance then the time for being completed also needs to be built in to the plan.MIDPs will need to be updated as new appointed parties join the delivery team (they might not all be appointed at the same time, particularly in a construction delivery team with a long sequence of sub-contract appointments), or when the project programme is revised (perhaps because of bad weather delaying groundworks, or manufacturing difficulties delaying site delivery and installation of some components). Because of this, it is helpful for the MIDP to be linked to (or even part of) the overall project programme.

Test information production methods and procedures

Mobilizing each delivery team has three key components: To make sure that the right people are equipped to do the work (both technically and in terms of their understanding of the information management process and the requirements on them)To make sure that the IT solutions work properly (this might involve remote connections from site and direct data feed from site teams to head office)To test the production methods and procedures, including checking that all geometrical information is produced in relation to a common origin point (which might have been specified by the client/appointing party)Testing the proposed arrangement of software platforms (not just the packages but also the actual versions/releases) that are going to be used. This is to make sure that the assumptions made prior to contract, that package A will exchange data with package B etc. actually works in practice. To do this, some realistic sample models need to be used in the trial exchanges. It is far better to find out in advance whether there are going to be any problems than to find out when the first real information exchange is being made and is found not to work. This is also the case if an intermediate format such as Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) is going to be used, to make sure that the import/export routines work as anticipated.Consideration should be given to whether the resulting information will be: Imported into another applicationFederated with other information possibly in other schemas andLinked with other deliverables (either a document or hyperlinks, or as sematic web links) Does the deliverable meet the needs of the recipients? Is information lost or distorted?Transfer in both directions should NOT be tested, NOR the round-trip exchange from package A to package B and then back to package A: The collaborative working method embedded in the use of a CDE (or direct transactional information delivery) eliminates this need.

Managing information during design and construction

This module revisits some of the key messages about managing the development and exchange of information during design and construction through the Common Data Environment (CDE). ISO 19650-1 defines the Common Data Environment in Clauses 11 and 12.This module also explores the practicalities of collaborative working that are needed within project delivery teams (design and construction).

Common Data Environment ISO 19650-1 Clauses 3.3.15, 11.1 and 12
Agreed source of information for any given project or asset, for collecting, managing and disseminating each information container through a managed process.
Project information goes through a series of checks and sign-offs to make sure it is suitable for its purpose.
To ensure that there is only one source of truth and that subsequent project work cannot be based on out-of-date information.

This slide shows the CDE concept described in ISO19650-1. The key features are:Project information exists in one of a number of ‘states’, which broadly equate to its degree of development towards a formal deliverableTransitions between one state and the next require checks and reviews to take placeWork In Progress (WIP) information is private to its originating organization/team and has the lowest status code. The originator can be any member of the project team – a design consultant, a contractor or sub-contractor, even a manufacturer responsible for a bespoke componentShared information is viewable, usable but not editable by other teams on the project and its use is limited by its status code. The permitted uses of any information within the CDE and its existence as the latest version are indicated through metadata attached to the filePublished information is the deliverable, usually available at/following the end of the project stageArchive is a journal of changes to the information in the CDE which can be used as an audit trail/history in case of disputeThe CDE is not limited to one physical computer system - it exists through the rules applied by those organizations working on the project. It is important to remember that it is the information originating organization/team that indicates the status code for their information, particularly for the different types of shared information. If another team/organization needs the same information for a different purpose, they have to ask the originator to re-issue it with the appropriate code. This is important for tracking responsibility and liability relating to information.The CDE solution (the software tool implementing the CDE process) will keep track of which is the latest revision of each file and by default should only allow those latest versions to be downloaded in the Shared state by other members of the team. Superseded files that are recoded in the Archive journal should only be accessible to administrators of the system.

This workflow is an example of the CDE process in action, using the information states to show how information is progressively developed during the work stage. This work stage can be part of the project Plan of Work, or can also represent an asset management activity where information is being produced.In project implementation of BIM, this workflow is now becoming commonplace within multi-disciplinary design teams. Typically, the workflow cycle (the main loop on the slide) operates over a two-week period. For very intense design development, this workflow is sometimes operated on a weekly cycle, but this is really only possible when the quantity and complexity of the issues to be resolved is relatively low after the review of the federated information.

Key6.1 check availability of reference information and shared resources6.2 generate information6.3 complete quality assurance check6.4 review information and approve for sharing6.5 information model reviewA information model progressed by subsequent delivery team(s) for each appointmentB new information container revision

This slide shows one of the expanded sub-processes from ISO 19650-2. In this case, this is the collaborative production of information (as we have been considering in these slides), which is in Clause 5.6.The subclauses within Clause 5.6 are illustrated in the flowchart. The three parallel flows are an illustration of three task teams working collaboratively within a delivery team. It is clear that this flowchart does not contain all of the complexity of the collaborative workflow, even for the simplified workflow shown on slide 57.The key tasks for each task team are as follows: In Clause 5.6.1, check that they have access to the relevant reference information and shared resources within the project’s common data environment.In Clause 5.6.2, generate their task team information in accordance with their respective TIDP. In doing so, make sure they comply with the project’s information standard, the project’s information production methods and procedures and meet the required level of information need. The task team also needs to make sure they are remaining within the appropriate part of the federation strategy or the allocated elements in the information container breakdown structure. This should ensure they do not duplicate information generated by other task teams. Finally, the task team has to make sure it is coordinating and cross-referencing its work with information shared within the common data environment, for example to spatially coordinate geometrical models with other geometrical models shared with the appropriate suitability.In Clause 5.6.3 and Clause 5.6.4, carry out checks on the information container (does it meet the information production methods and procedures, e.g. in using the correct file name) and then review the information in the container. This content review would include comparing the contents against the EIR and the level of information need. If both sets of checks are passed then the container is approved for sharing.Once all the task team containers have been shared, then the delivery team as a whole can review the federated information model – this is in Clause 5.6.5. This is largely about coordinating information across the different task teams.

Revision and status are required to be managed in a CDE.UK: The format of the status and revision codes are specified in BS EN ISO 19650-2 Revised National Annex (revision issued February 2021). The relevant clauses are NA.4.2 and NA.4.3 and projects ‘should’ comply with these specifications. In a National Annex ‘should’ is the strongest term allowed without changing the meaning of the ISO. The iterative production of information can be explained in Revision and Status terms as follows:In the style Pnn.nn (preliminary revisions)In WIP, start at P01.01Status is S0, meaning work in progressSuccessive versions prior to sharing become P01.02, P01.03, etc.In SHARED, upload as revision P01 (i.e. drop the numerals after the decimal point)Status is S1…S5, the meaning of each status code is described in the table in National Annex Clause NA.4.2 – reproduced on following slideDuring the collaborative production workflow (see slide 58), download other disciplines’ information, update own WIP information and revisions become P02.01, P02.02, etc.Re-upload to SHARED as P02Repeat as required, increasing the revision code each timeIn the style Cnn (contractual revisions)When SHARED information is first authorized for publication, revision changes to C01Further issues of same contractual information are C02, C03, etc.Either way, when information is authorized into PUBLISHED stateStatus may become A[stage_number] meaning authorized/accepted for the stage Status may become B[stage_number] meaning ‘authorized/accepted but…’ for this stage (minor revisions pending) – however the use of B codes for partial acceptance/authorization is deprecated in the revised National AnnexBecause the A or B codes are tied to project stage numbers rather than a particular purpose of issue then the Project Information Standard should document the “meaning” of each A/B code (e.g. to confirm that code A4 is to be used for construction or that code A5 is to be used for handover information)

Status codesThese are required to ensure that the receivers of information understand what each document can be used for and at which stage. The codes are applied to each information container (document, schedule, model etc.) within the CDE prior to authorization for release. They should be applied as metadata within the container (file), not appended to the file name. Whilst it may be difficult to determine which status code should be used, the work stage you are currently in or if the supplier you are issuing the information to will support the process. Status codes are used on a stage-by-stage basis throughout a project, so an information container (e.g. an equipment schedule) that is being developed through two stages will go through the appropriate status codes twice as the “Published” version from the first stage is further developed as “Work in Progress” in the second stage.Note: Metadata is information that is about an object or resource that describes characteristics such as content, quality, format, location or status.
ISO 19650 Part 2: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Project Delivery Phase Training Course
BS EN ISO 19650-2 UK National Annex (revised 2021) sets out a clear system of naming information containers:

ISO 19650-2 Clause 5.1.7 a) requires that each information container has a unique ID (name in the CDE solution/system). An information container is defined in ISO 19650-1 Clause 3.3.12 as: A named persistent set of information retrievable from a file, system or application storage hierarchyAn information container could be a sub-directory, information file (such as a model, document, table, etc.) or distinct sub-set of an information file, such as a chapter, section, layer or symbol. The term information container is not widely used in general conversation, but it was important to find a term that could cover the broad range of instances. Containers can be large or small.UK: File naming should be in line with the convention defined in the UK National Annex to ISO 19650-2 revised in 2021. Other types of information containers such as layers have their own naming convention.The example file name shown on the slide indicates that this an information container (file) for project PR1, produced by XYZ, concerning system S1, relating to 3rd floor (or location 3), the file is a drawing (D) and relates to Civil Engineering, and it is the first such container with all of those previous codes. If another file had all the same codes, it would be numbered 0002, and so on.There are standard codes for some of the fields in the container name (particularly Form and Discipline), others are suggested to be referenced to tables in Uniclass 2015 (particularly Functional Breakdown), and others are created by the project parties (Project and Originator).

“BIM according to ISO 19650” requires existing project team members to do something different in the way they carry out project design and construction. New functions and activities have been specified in ISO 19650-2 but the way that these are carried out by people within the project, delivery and task teams is not spelt out in the ISO.This module reviews the potential roles arising from these functions and activities.It is entirely possible that BIM roles are carried out by existing team members. Indeed, this is strongly recommended, provided those individuals have the appropriate knowledge and skill. BIM is not intended to be a job-title creation exercise.Secondly, BIM activities are supposed to be performed instead of the wasteful repetition of design and construction and unnecessary fire-fighting to solve problems. The premise of BIM is that there will be a net saving of time over the totality of design and construction, as problems are caught earlier and don’t escalate to become major, expensive items of rework.

Assigning responsibility for aspects of the information management functions can be done using Annex A in ISO 19650-2. The assignment matrix in Table A.1 is high-level, as the rows align to the separate clauses in ISO 19650-2. A more detailed breakdown of activities might be needed to properly allocate accountability and responsibility, and to record which parties will be informed or consulted.The different types of party mentioned in ISO 19650-2 are named in the columns of the assignment matrix, but on a particular project each column should state the name of each relevant organisation. The Lead Appointed Party and Appointed Party columns will need to be duplicated to cover each of the Lead Appointed Parties and each of the Appointed Parties. The Third party column is to be used when the appointing party is delegating some or all of its activities to an external consultant.

The involvement that is needed from the appointing party covers: Information management: They need to understand their own governance process for projectsInformation requirements management: They need to collect and document the information requirements from their stakeholders. These are roles that will be needed at the beginning of BIM implementation on a projectInformation delivery management: Information deliverables provided by the delivery teams will need to be reviewed, authorized and accepted, and someone in the appointing party team will need to take responsibility for this. There is also the coordination between delivery teams that needs to be overseenIt is entirely possible, and allowable in the ISO, that the appointing party does not have the skills and capacity to carry out all these functions. In this case, the appointing party can delegate aspects of its information management function to a specialist appointed party, or to one or more of the lead appointed parties involved in the project. However, if it does this, the appointing party must remember that it remains accountable for these activities.Given the range of activities that appointing parties (clients) are likely to need to carry out, or to be responsible for, there are some corresponding skills and capabilities that their staff need to have to be successful in BIM projects. These are in addition to the technical project management skills that they already need.These skills and capabilities are not just focused on information management – these client roles also need to bridge across into understanding the business operations of the client organization.

This slide focuses on the lead appointed party.Within each delivery team, someone will be responsible for managing the coordination process (policing the collaboration workflow) and someone will be responsible for managing the information process for the project. During early project stages, it is likely that these roles will be found within the design lead organization. On a building project this is likely to be the architect, on an infrastructure project this is likely to be the civil or structural engineer, on a project driven by services such as a data centre this might be the Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) engineer.During later project stages, the coordination manager and information manager are likely to be found in the contractor lead organization – the main contractor, or Design and Build contractor etc.The project-wide roles are those of coordination management and information management. Like all other roles, these are not necessarily carried out by two separate people, or even two new people. Both can be delivered, with suitable training and knowledge, by existing project management staff. How these roles are resourced will be a project-by-project decision.Information management provides the project focus for the procedures and processes that are used to handle information. This includes making sure that the admin procedures for file naming, assigning status and revision codes, setting model origin points and so on are all carried out correctly and consistently across the whole project team. Where team members are working together for the first time, or for the first time on a BIM project, then some training and mentoring may be needed. The information manager is the ideal role to provide this, but may elect to source training from outside the project team, for example by using specialist trainers.
ISO 19650 Part 2: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Project Delivery Phase Training Course
Coordination management provides the technical focus for resolving coordination issues that arise as project information is produced.
This role is more about the workflow of different design teams following the timetable of uploads, downloads, attending coordination reviews and implementing agreed changes.

The federation of the separate task information models is likely to be done as part of coordination management, and the review meetings to consider the issues are likely to be chaired by the coordination manager.In the UK and countries having a similar legal frameworks, the CIC publication on the scope of services for information management is a helpful guide to the role of the information manager.

Task information management (process and content review)

Under the delivery team roles, there are the task teams. As well as producing the design and construction information (originators), there will be a need to liaise with other task teams when design clashes are identified. There is also the oversight of the information management process, checking the deliverables shared onto the CDE, making sure that everyone in the team is following the procedures, and representing the team at information management meetings.In many task teams, the task information manager may be combined with the team manager role.Many of the activities of task information management have been discussed in other parts of this course, but the key activities are:Developing the task information delivery plan, and keeping it up to date if the project changesMaking sure that everyone in the task team understands the volumes that have been agreed with the rest of the project team for their particular design elementsBeing proactive to solve coordination problems caused by design elements straying outside the agreed volumes. Where these occur, they should be addressed at the lowest level of the project hierarchy and only escalated to higher levels if a solution cannot be agreed

The skills and capabilities within the supply chain involve both technical skill and knowledge for production of project information (design standards, construction methods) as well as project management knowledge. This includes understanding of the project management processes being used to manage information (i.e. the ISO 19650-1 principles and the ISO 19650-2 requirements).Information management requires particular knowledge of the CDE and its associated processes. Coordination management requires a focus on technical design and the practicalities of system design within the project disciplines.Task information management needs similar skills to information management, and the interface manager is parallel to the skillset of coordination management.Information originator skills are mainly about translating engineering or architectural design into information deliverables (models, drawings, schedules, etc.) This does not have to be a separate team of people from the engineers, architects. In fact, part of the change in digitizing construction is to combine design and construction expertise with the ability to use the information production tools. Clearly this all needs to be done in a way that is compatible with ISO 19650-1 and ISO 19650-2 processes.

This module summarizes the key concepts and messages from the course, and also gives you the chance to action plan your next steps. There are many important points that have been raised during the course today. In this module it is impossible to revisit all of them, but this slide sets out some of the key points.Your understanding of these key points, and the work that your organization may already have done, will help you prioritize which aspects of ISO 19650-2 need attention first.ISO 19650-2 exists within a framework of other BIM standards – there are many crossovers between the standardsThe information delivery cycle requires both client-side and supply-side commitment and action – it is no help for a client to just ask for a project to be ‘BIM’ and in fact this is completely incompatible with the ISO 19650 approachPIRs and EIRs capture the appointing party (UK: Client) requirementsBEP, TIDPs and MIDP capture the appointed party (UK: Supply chain) responseAppointed party capability is demonstrated through various assessments at the time of appointmentCDE processes are defined in ISO 19650-1 Roles and responsibilities exist on both sides of the appointment – who will be your information manager, coordination manager, etc.?
